content:
{
  "title": "Executable Specification Prompting: Code Generation from Formalized Behavioral Descriptions",
  "problem": "LLMs often misinterpret ambiguous or underspecified prompts in code generation, leading to incorrect or suboptimal outputs. There is a need for prompting techniques that more precisely convey the required behavior and constraints for code tasks.",
  "motivation": "Ambiguity in natural language prompts limits LLM code generation reliability, especially as tasks grow in complexity or specificity. While prior work improves reasoning or introduces programmatic prompt structures, few methods leverage formalized, testable behavioral specifications as an explicit part of the prompt. This is timely given the interest in alignment and rigorous code correctness from industry and research.",
  "method": "We propose Executable Specification Prompting (ESP): prompts are augmented with a concise, language-agnostic formal description of the expected input-output behavior (e.g., property-based test specifications or pseudo-code assertions) alongside the natural language task. The LLM is instructed to generate code that will pass these specifications, with the behavioral description written in a uniform, model-agnostic syntax. ESP differs from prior work by requiring the LLM to align its generation with an executable, checkable specification rather than just a natural language or chain-of-thought rationale.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate ESP on HumanEval, MBPP, and APPS, comparing against chain-of-thought, plan-based, and modular prompting baselines. Metrics include functional correctness, specification coverage, and pass@k. Ablations will test the impact of different specification granularities and syntaxes."
}
